labels.rationale: Figure A includes all the correct labels from the reference (O, p, P, and their derivatives), while Figure B is missing several key labels like O and P.

concept.rationale: Figure B constructs a true 3D camera geometry where the viewing ray mathematically intersects the image plane, whereas Figure A incorrectly fakes the diagram by placing all points on a single flat plane that doesn't actually intersect the drawn image plane in 3D space.

aggregator.explanation: While Figure A is more faithful to the original's superficial traits like labels and colors, Figure B constructs a vastly superior true 3D mathematical model of the camera geometry and features richer interactivity. Figure A fundamentally fails geometrically by faking a 3D diagram on a flat 2D plane, making Figure B the stronger educational tool.

geometry.rationale: Figure B implements true 3D spatial geometry with proper perspective projection, whereas Figure A hardcodes a flattened 2D drawing onto a single plane in 3D space.

interactivity.rationale: Figure B includes functional sliders and step-through animations that modify the 3D scene, providing meaningful pedagogical interaction, whereas Figure A only relies on tooltips and standard camera controls.

faithfulness.rationale: Figure A accurately reproduces the blue color of the points, correct placement of the rotation arrows, and the scale of the local green axes, whereas Figure B has black points, misplaced arrows, and visible UI sliders.

labels.rationale: Figure A accurately reproduces the placement, styling, and relative sizing of all labels, whereas Figure B has severe positioning errors for the rotational labels (Yaw, Pitch, θy, θx) that clutter the scene and fail to match the original.

concept.rationale: Figure B correctly implements the mathematical relationship between 3D point rotation and 2D perspective projection, allowing students to interactively learn the concepts through functional sliders.

aggregator.explanation: Although Figure A is more faithful to the original aesthetic design and label placement, it relies on a hardcoded, flattened 2D drawing. Figure B is the stronger overall choice because it implements true 3D mathematical geometry and perspective projection, offering functional sliders and animations that meaningfully convey the underlying pedagogical concepts.

geometry.rationale: Both figures incorrectly implement the 3D camera geometry as a flattened 2D diagram in the XY plane, but Figure A is better due to its interactive representation of depth along the back-projection ray.

interactivity.rationale: Figure A provides meaningful developer-built interactions, including a functional depth slider and 3D point dragging that dynamically update the projection geometry, whereas Figure B is entirely static besides basic tooltips.

faithfulness.rationale: Figure A correctly places the origin and destination of the curved arrows at the red points p and p', matching the reference, whereas Figure B incorrectly connects them to the dashed projection lines.

labels.rationale: Figure B has significantly better label placement, avoiding the severe text-object overlapping seen in Figure A (especially on the red dots).

concept.rationale: Figure A includes an interactive slider that dynamically demonstrates how changing depth $z$ slides the lifted 3D point along its back-projection ray and moves the projected correspondence $p'$ in the reference frame, actively teaching the core geometric relationship.

aggregator.explanation: Figure A is the clear overall winner due to its highly effective interactivity and dynamic demonstration of the core geometric concepts, alongside better faithfulness to the reference diagram. While Figure B has superior label placement, Figure A's functional depth slider and draggable points provide significant pedagogical value that outweighs its overlapping text issues.

geometry.rationale: Figure A constructs a smooth multi-segment gradient for the incoming light ray and accurately matches the static layout and element counts of the standard BRDF diagram, whereas Figure B uses a much coarser two-cylinder approximation for the ray and introduces extraneous lobe geometries.

interactivity.rationale: Figure B includes functional sliders and a dropdown to interactively adjust the light angle, view angle, and BRDF type, whereas Figure A only relies on click-to-read popups.

faithfulness.rationale: Figure A reproduces the multiple semi-transparent scattered rays from the reference, whereas Figure B incorrectly substitutes them with a single specular lobe and includes extraneous UI controls.

labels.rationale: Figure A accurately replicates the mathematical typography of the reference by using the correct script 'ell' and bold, non-italic font for vectors p, n, and q, whereas Figure B incorrectly italicizes these vectors.

concept.rationale: Figure B interactively models the BRDF lobes (specular and Lambertian) and allows students to manipulate incoming and outgoing angles, brilliantly demonstrating both the directional dependence and cosine falloff.

aggregator.explanation: Although Figure B offers excellent interactivity and conceptual modeling of BRDF lobes, Figure A is the overall winner due to its superior geometry, precise mathematical typography, and strict faithfulness to the reference diagram. Figure A accurately reproduces the complex scattered rays and standard layout, making it a more faithful recreation despite lacking the dynamic UI of Figure B.
